questions.description: Write a brief note on the historical influence of ANY TWO of the following on modern Irish land law: (a) Statute of…
questions.text: Write a brief note on the historical influence of ANY TWO of the following on modern Irish land law: (a) Statute of Westminster III (Quia Emptores) 1290; (b) Tenure Abolitions Act 1662; (c) Registration of Deeds Act 1707; (d) Fines and Recoveries (Ireland) Act 1834.

questions.description: De Londras (Principles of Irish Property Law, 2007 at p.146) has observed: "When they were introduced the Settled Land…
questions.text: De Londras (Principles of Irish Property Law, 2007 at p.146) has observed: "When they were introduced the Settled Land Acts 1882-1890 represented a major development on behalf of holders of limited estates (i.e. life estates and fee tails, in particular). However, the law relating to settled land now appears to have little or no application." What is your opinion of this perspective on the Settled Land Acts? Support your answer with relevant case law, principle and commentary.

questions.description: According to the website of the Property Registration Authority, "A State-guaranteed system of title registration such…
questions.text: According to the website of the Property Registration Authority, "A State-guaranteed system of title registration such as that operated in Ireland is an important instrument for the protection of property owners and the facilitation of innumerable financial transactions which are the hallmarks of a modern market economy." Taking that statement into account, outline and assess critically the system for recording transactions in relation to property in Ireland.

questions.description: Write a note on ANY TWO of the following aspects of Irish land law: (a) Rights and responsibilities of joint tenants as…
questions.text: Write a note on ANY TWO of the following aspects of Irish land law: (a) Rights and responsibilities of joint tenants as co-owners; (b) Rights and duties of cohabitants in relation to property; (c) Rights of residence: (d) Rights of successors-in-title and freehold covenants.

questions.description: In 1993, the Munster Development Corporation (MDC), a statutory body… Advise Ray.
questions.text: In 1993, the Munster Development Corporation (MDC), a statutory body charged with the promotion of industrial developments in the province, purchased a field near Limerick City with the intention of selling it in the future with the benefit of planning permission. The purchase of the field made the local newspapers and media because it was felt at the time the MDC had paid well over the going market rate for the field. In June 1995, cattle belonging to the neighbouring farmer, Eoin, broke into the field. Eoin noticed that the fencing on the field was in bad repair and that the land appeared to be lying idle. Eoin decided there and then to leave his cattle to graze on the field. A few months later, Eoin repaired the fence and was soon maintaining the fencing on a regular basis, as well as spreading fertilizer on the land. In 2000, Eoin carried out expensive drainage work on the land. In 2002, he retired from farming and transferred whatever right, title or interest he had in the field to his son, Ray, who has been farming the land since. In June 2007, MDC put up a site notice indicating that a planning permission application for the erection of a factory had been lodged with the local authority. Ray recently noticed a "For Sale" sign on the property and wrote to the estate agents involved with an offer to purchase the property. He has recently heard that he may have "squatter's rights" to the property, and seeks legal advice on the matter. Advise Ray.

Answer either (a) OR (b). (a) In the context of Irish law on mortgages generally, discuss critically the creation, effect and possible reform of judgment mortgages. [OR]{.underline} (b) In the context of Irish law on easements generally, discuss critically the creation, effect and possible reform rights of way and the right to light and the right to support.

questions.description: In McGinness v Sherry & Anor [2008] IEHC 134, the High Court considered an application seeking directions as to the…
questions.text: In McGinness v Sherry & Anor [2008] IEHC 134, the High Court considered an application seeking directions as to the precise meaning and import of a bequest contained in a will. On reviewing the "several rules of construction" used by the Irish courts in the process of interpreting the terms of a will, Mac Menamin J cited a specific rule which suggested: "Where it is clear on the fact [or face] of a will that the testator has not accurately or completely expressed his meaning by the words he has used, and it is also clear what are the words he has omitted, those words may be supplied in order to affect the intention [of the testator], as collected from the context." Given the above: (a) Outline and discuss generally the various rules of construction that are used by the Irish courts in attempting to give effect to the ambiguous terms of a will or imprecise parts thereof; [and]{.underline} (b) In your answer, make particular reference to the balance that must be struck between an interpretation that simply removes a possible ambiguity in a will and that which might involve the re-writing of a will.

questions.description: Joe McEvoy made his will in June 2004. Advise Marie.
questions.text: Joe McEvoy made his will in June 2004. He died in July 2007, survived by his two sons, Daniel and Martin, and his daughter Marie. Probate of his will was granted in July 2008. By that will, John made the following bequests: "My 50 acre farm at Blackacre and my house at Black Hill to my son Daniel absolutely. My land at Whiteacre to my son Daniel absolutely but my daughter Marie may take a site from this property for the purpose of building a house there on. It is my wish that my daughter would not sell this site. My bank account at Eire Bank, I leave to my son Martin absolutely. All the residue and remainder of my estate to be equally divided between my two sons and daughter." In 2004, the house at Black Hill and the adjacent land at Blackacre were valued at €200,000. It is now valued at €270,000. The land at Whiteacre which is adjacent to the River Shannon is a small holding of no more than two acres. It was valued at €65,000 in 2004 and is now valued at €75,000. The bank account amounts to €50,000. There was no residue to divide, as it went on funeral expenses. Daniel, the youngest child, is now in his mid-thirties, and lives alone at Black Hill. He has suffered from regular bouts of depression since childhood but a recent change in medication appears to be working well. The farmland at Blackacre is not of good quality, and Daniel is grateful to supplement his income with his weekly disability benefit. Martin has been living in San Francisco for the past ten years. Thanks in no small part to an investment of €15,000 made by his later father in 1998 Martin's hairdressing business is thriving. He kept in regular contact with his father and remains on good terms with Daniel but has not spoken to Marie since the unexpected death of their mother in 1999. Marie is unhappy with the outcome of the will and writes to you to seek advice on challenging its terms. In the letter she informs you of what she calls "four points of interest". First, she says, although her father funded her education, which led her to her career as a social worker, she has not seen eye-to-eye with him since 1999. She thinks that this may have had to do with the fact that she refused to return to the family home when her mother died. Second, she notes that since her father died her diabetes has dis-improved appreciably due to a combination of work-related stress and a "slight" drink problem. Third, she claims that the site at Whiteacre "isn't worth a curse" due to the fact that it is prone to flooding. Finally, she includes in her letter a clipping from a recent edition of the local newspaper, which reports that the route of the planned motorway through the locality is likely to be adjusted in order to avoid the ruins of an old Abbey, and that a number of local farmers appear set to benefit from the "windfall" of the various compulsory purchases orders that will in all likelihood be needed to develop the road. From the plans published in the paper, it now appears that the motorway will edge onto Blackwater. Her letter ends by stating that, although she would not like to "unduly upset" Daniel, he is now "sitting on a goldmine" and that this is "clearly unfair". Advise Marie.

questions.description: Write a brief note on the continuing influence, if any, on modern Irish land law of ANY TWO of the following: (a) The…
questions.text: Write a brief note on the continuing influence, if any, on modern Irish land law of ANY TWO of the following: (a) The concept of feudal tenure and the ownership of land; (b) The creation of a fee farm grant at law or in equity; (c) The creation of a fee tail at law or in equity; (d) The operation of future interests in land, whether vested or contingent.

questions.description: The Residential Tenancies Act 2004 contains far-reaching reforms of the private rented sector in Ireland.
questions.text: The Residential Tenancies Act 2004 contains far-reaching reforms of the private rented sector in Ireland. With regard to the general scheme of the Act -- for example, its provisions on improved security of tenure; its new tenancy termination procedures; and the establishment of the Private Residential Tenancies Board as a dispute resolution mechanism -- critically assess whether the Residential Tenancies Act 2004 has succeeded in its objective of providing a clear statutory framework for the tenancy obligation of landlords and tenants in Ireland.

questions.description: Betty was the fee simple owner in freehold of The Pines, a large house… Advise Patricia.
questions.text: Betty was the fee simple owner in freehold of The Pines, a large house with extensive surrounding grounds. Betty used The Pines as a bed and breakfast business. In 1999, a local businessman, Tom, asked Betty if she might be interested in selling part of the grounds attached to The Pines. Tom intended to build a restaurant on the land. Betty was agreeable but in the conveyance with Tom she ensured that Tom covenanted on behalf of himself and his heirs and his assigns that he would refrain from using the premises for anything other than a restaurant. The conveyance, which was registered, also contained a covenant requiring Tom to maintain the driveway shared by the properties. Finally, it was expressly stated in the conveyance that the covenants were made "for the benefit of Betty and all future owners of The Pines". In December 2006, Betty sold The Pines to Patricia. In April 2007, Tom sold the restaurant to his nephew, Peter. Peter, anxious to develop the business, began to provide live music for patrons, which, on summer nights, soon turned into a feature on the restaurant's business and regularly led to impromptu parties being held on the restaurant's decking/patio area. Patricia's customers began to complain about the noise and recently one of them posted a long commentary on a popular travel website advising that it was now probably best to avoid Patricia's B&B during the holiday season. In addition, the customer noted that generally standards had fallen at Patricia's B&B with the driveway now looking no better than a "forgotten country lane". Last week, after a particularly raucous "theme night" at the restaurant, Patricia decided to confront Peter. She reminded him: "when you bought that place from your uncle you signed up to the same obligations as he had." Peter coolly replied: "the only thing I signed was the cheque to buy this restaurant and if the noise is bothering your customers then you should invest in double glazing." Advise Patricia.

questions.description: Write a brief note on ANY TWO of the followings "rights" in Irish land law: (a) The right to treasure trove; (b) The…
questions.text: Write a brief note on ANY TWO of the followings "rights" in Irish land law: (a) The right to treasure trove; (b) The right to survivorship; (c) The right to profit a prendre from the land of another; (d) The right to a bare licence.

questions.description: Daniel owns the fee simple in a house in Co. Mortgage.
questions.text: Daniel owns the fee simple in a house in Co. Cork. The property, which is Daniel's only asset and is unregistered, was valued at €300,000 when Daniel inherited it from his uncle in 2006. In the winter of 2008 after a long night in the local pub, Daniel fell into a conversation with the local auctioneer, James. James told Daniel that he was involved in a new retail development in the city, and that it was a "sure thing" that money would be made from the project. James recommended that Daniel should invest at least €400,000 in the scheme, and that Daniel should approach a number of local banks to see if they might lend him the money. The next day Daniel made an appointment with his local bank, Fitzpatrick's Lenders, and asked for a loan of €300,000. The bank manager, Sean, agreed and asked Daniel to deposit the title deeds to his house with Fitzpatrick's as security for the loan. Daniel deposited the title deeds with Fitzpatrick's but a month later he called Sean and told him that he needed the title deeds for his solicitor as he was drawing up his will. Sean gave the deeds to Daniel. Daniel drove to Limerick and approached Easicredit Bank for a mortgage of €100,000. The manager, Breffni, glanced at the title deeds and asked Daniel if there were any other mortgages secured on the property. "No, thank God" replied Daniel. Breffni agreed to approve the mortgage, and Daniel signed a mortgage deed to secure the loan with Easicredit. Daniel transferred the €400,000 to James in June of this year but has not been able to contact James since August when James said that he was going away for a "short break". Daniel has been struggling to meet the mortgage repayments to Fitzpatrick's and Easicredit. In fact, he has not made any repayments since August. Sean and Breffni are worried about their exposure to the loans involved. Advise the banks as to their respective rights in law.

Answer (a) [AND]{.underline} (b). (a) Explain the role that the Partition Acts 1868-1876 (sections 3 and 4 in particular) play in the termination of the co-ownership of land. [AND]{.underline} (b) Explain the role that the Statute of Limitations 1957 (sections 13 and 24 in particular) has in regulating the law on the adverse possession of land.

questions.description: In H v O'C and others [2007] IEHC 68, Herbert J stated that in determining… Succession.
questions.text: In H v O'C and others [2007] IEHC 68, Herbert J stated that in determining issues arising in applications pursuant to section 117 of the Succession Act 1965 the High Court must: - act justly, fairly and impartially, to avoid the greatest extent possible perpetuating or deepening any feelings of suspicion or hostility which the litigation had engendered among family members; and - remember that the test to be applied was not which of the various courses open to the testator the court itself would have adopted if confronted with the same situation, but rather, whether the decision of the testator to opt for the course did of itself, and without more, constitute a breach of moral duty to the claimant. What do you think of Herbert J's approach to section 117 applications? Support your answer with reference to relevant case law, principle and commentary.

questions.description: Henry had been seriously ill for some weeks before he finally died in June…
questions.text: Henry had been seriously ill for some weeks before he finally died in June of this year. In his will, which he made in 2006, he left his entire estate (worth €1.5 million) to his wife Karen and his son Hector in equal shares. When making his will in 2006, Henry had been estranged from his daughter Patti, a single mother, who lived in a commune in Mayo. In the Christmas before he died, Henry had a chance encounter with Patti, and her son Eoin. The meeting deeply affected Henry and he sought to amend his will. Henry's solicitor, Bertie, advised him that he did not need to make a completely new will and that a codicil would suffice in the circumstances. Henry duly instructed Bertie to draft a codicil leaving €100,000 to Patti. Barite managed to have the codicil ready by the end of May of this year. By that stage, Henry was hospitalised and under heavy medication. Barite advised the hospital to get Henry to sign the codicil. The nurse on duty expressed some doubts as to whether Henry would know what was about to happen. Barite, who is a solicitor of 30 years' standing, politely acknowledged her advice and then asked the nurse to witness the will. On seeing Bertie, Henry appeared to rally himself and signed his name to the codicil in Bertie's presence. While this was going on the nurse was called away to tend to another patient and only returned after Bertie had signed his name. Following Henry's death, a family dispute arose. Patti was disappointed that she was left with less than 10% of Henry's estate, and that nothing had been passed to Eoin as Henry's only grandchild. Hector was livid that Patti had received so much, and has told a friend that he suspects "emotional blackmail" on Patti's part. Karen is concerned that the family home (worth €700,000) will have to be sold in order to give Hector and Patti their share of the estate. Karen seeks legal advice. Advise Karen on the validity of the will and codicil and on any other issues that may affect the distribution of Henry's estate.
